title: if and switch

canonicalExplanation.summary: Java if statements and switch statements or expressions choose one documented path from complete conditions, with case coverage, fall-through, yields, and null behavior made explicit.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A score classifier tests the exact lower and upper boundaries for each band and uses an else branch for invalid input. Your goal is to implement and test boundary-complete branching including enum or string cases. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: First source and main; pf state expressions; Operators and conversions. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Java if statements and switch statements or expressions choose one documented path from complete conditions, with case coverage, fall-through, yields, and null behavior made explicit. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Classic switch statements can fall through, switch expressions must produce a value, enum/string/null handling differs by construct and version, and boundary predicates can leave gaps. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Every Java switch case stops automatically and default always makes a decision complete and correct.

includes: Use statements and expressions for complete, readable decisions.
Implement and test boundary-complete branching including enum or string cases.
excludes: Classic switch statements can fall through, switch expressions must produce a value, enum/string/null handling differs by construct and version, and boundary predicates can leave gaps.

outcomes: Implement and test boundary-complete branching including enum or string cases.

examples.title: if and switch: direct language case
examples.situation: A score classifier tests the exact lower and upper boundaries for each band and uses an else branch for invalid input.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A score classifier tests the exact lower and upper boundaries for each band and uses an else branch for invalid input.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Use arrow cases or explicit control transfer to prevent accidental fall-through, and test every boundary rather than treating default as proof of complete selection.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Use arrow cases or explicit control transfer to prevent accidental fall-through, and test every boundary rather than treating default as proof of complete selection.

examples.title: if and switch: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A switch expression over an enum returns one label per constant and uses a deliberate default only when forward compatibility is more important than exhaustive compiler checking.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A switch expression over an enum returns one label per constant and uses a deliberate default only when forward compatibility is more important than exhaustive compiler checking.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Classic switch statements can fall through, switch expressions must produce a value, enum/string/null handling differs by construct and version, and boundary predicates can leave gaps.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Every Java switch case stops automatically and default always makes a decision complete and correct.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement and test boundary-complete branching including enum or string cases.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Every Java switch case stops automatically and default always makes a decision complete and correct.
misconceptions.correction: Use arrow cases or explicit control transfer to prevent accidental fall-through, and test every boundary rather than treating default as proof of complete selection.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Every Java switch case stops automatically and default always makes a decision complete and correct.

analogy.example: a control panel whose typed gauges, expressions, switches, and repeating cycles produce observable state can introduce if and switch, after the canonical Java: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: tracing primitive values, operators, decisions, and loop progress
limitations: the panel does not model promotion, overflow, short-circuit evaluation, scope, or JVM representation exactly

recap.summary: You should now be able to implement and test boundary-complete branching including enum or string cases. Keep this boundary in view: Classic switch statements can fall through, switch expressions must produce a value, enum/string/null handling differs by construct and version, and boundary predicates can leave gaps.
retrievalPrompts: Explain if and switch using canonical Java: Beginner to Intermediate terms and one observable consequence.
Why is this belief incorrect: Every Java switch case stops automatically and default always makes a decision complete and correct.

Predict this case from memory: A switch expression over an enum returns one label per constant and uses a deliberate default only when forward compatibility is more important than exhaustive compiler checking.
